bytes.topic: OWASP Top 10 — injection, broken auth, XSS, IDOR, security misconfig and the rest
bytes.title: snyk - Securing Your Dependencies Against OWASP Top 10
bytes.body: Snyk's vulnerability database contains numerous threats from OWASP Top 10. To secure dependencies, monitor vulnerabilities in your open-source libraries and packages. Use snyk test to scan your project and identify potential security risks such as SQL injection, cross-site scripting (XSS), and security misconfiguration. For instance, if a library is vulnerable to SQL injection, Snyk's test will flag it and recommend a patch. This ensures that your application remains secure against common web application vulnerabilities. To further harden your application, enable snyk monitor to track new vulnerabilities as they emerge.

bytes.topic: SQL Injection — parameterized queries, ORMs as defense, testing for injection
bytes.title: snyk: Shielding Your Apps from SQL Injection with Parameterized Queries and ORMs
bytes.body: When using Snyk's Vulnerability Database, SQL Injection vulnerabilities often surface due to unparameterized queries. To mitigate this, use parameterized queries in your Snyk-based applications. Snyk recommends employing Object-Relational Mapping (ORM) tools like Snyk's own ORM, which automatically parameterizes queries. You can also utilize Snyk's Open Source Security testing to identify SQL Injection vulnerabilities and apply fixes. For example, with Snyk's CLI, you can run `snyk test` to detect vulnerabilities and `snyk apply` to apply fixes.

bytes.topic: XSS — reflected vs stored vs DOM-based, CSP headers, output encoding
bytes.title: Securing Your App with Snyk: XSS Variants and CSP Headers
bytes.body: Snyk's Vulnerability Database helps you identify Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerabilities in your dependencies. When analyzing an application, Snyk classifies XSS vulnerabilities into three categories: Reflected, Stored, and DOM-based. Reflected XSS occurs when user input is directly reflected in the application's response. Stored XSS happens when user input is stored in a database and later executed by the application. DOM-based XSS occurs when user input is executed in the application's client-side JavaScript. To mitigate Stored and Reflected XSS, Snyk recommends using Content-Security-Policy (CSP) headers, which specify allowed sources for certain types of content. You can configure CSP headers in Snyk's Policy Manager. To encode user input and prevent XSS, use Snyk's `encode` function, which can be accessed through the `snyk.security.html.encode` API.

bytes.topic: Security Misconfigurations — default credentials, exposed debug endpoints, verbose errors
bytes.title: Snyk: Detecting Default Credentials and Exposed Debug Endpoints
bytes.body:
Snyk's Vulnerability DB contains many vulnerabilities caused by default credentials and exposed debug endpoints. To detect these issues, use the `snyk test` command with the `--default-credentials` and `--exposed-endpoints` flags. For example:
snyk test --default-credentials --exposed-endpoints 
  --file=package.json
  --project=example-project

This will scan your project for vulnerabilities and report any findings.

bytes.topic: Authentication Flaws — weak passwords, credential stuffing, MFA, session management
bytes.title: Snyk: Strengthening Authentication with MFA and Session Management
bytes.body: When using Snyk's Open-Source Security (OSS) tool to scan dependencies, ensure your application's authentication mechanisms are secure. Weak passwords and credential stuffing attacks can be prevented by implementing Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA). Snyk's MFA support enables users to register and authenticate with a time-based one-time password (TOTP) or a Universal 2nd Factor (U2F) key. For session management, use Snyk's 'snyk policy init' command to generate a policy that includes the 'snyk.security.session-management' rule, ensuring proper session expiration and secure cookie flags.

bytes.topic: DAST — dynamic testing with Burp Suite, ZAP, testing running applications
bytes.title: Snyk — Automating DAST with Burp Suite and ZAP
bytes.body: To integrate dynamic application security testing (DAST) with Snyk, utilize Snyk's custom scripts feature. Snyk supports Burp Suite and ZAP as DAST tools. Create a custom script with the `snyk dast` command, specifying the tool and target application. For example, to run a ZAP scan, use `snyk dast zap --target https://example.com`. You can also configure the script to run scans on a schedule using Snyk's CI/CD integrations.
bytes.code_snippet:
snyk dast zap --target https://example.com
snyk dast zap --target https://example.com --schedule '* * * * * *'

bytes.topic: SAST — static analysis with Semgrep, SonarQube, integrating in CI pipeline
bytes.title: Integrate SAST with Semgrep in snyk
bytes.body: To integrate SAST with Semgrep in snyk, navigate to the 'SAST' tab in the snyk UI and click on 'Add SAST provider.' Select 'Semgrep' from the list of available providers. In the 'Semgrep configuration' section, enter your Semgrep API token and specify the repositories to scan. snyk will then use Semgrep to scan your code for security vulnerabilities.

bytes.topic: IDOR & Access Control — always validate server-side, never trust client for authorization
bytes.title: Snyk: Always Validate Server-Side Access Control with Policy as Code
bytes.body: When implementing access control in Snyk, never trust the client for authorization. Instead, validate server-side access control using policy as code. This ensures that even if a malicious actor manipulates the client, your server remains secure. With Snyk's Policy as Code feature, you can define granular permissions and scope them to specific repositories or environments. Use the `snyk policy` command to manage and enforce these policies across your organization.

bytes.topic: Burp Suite — intercepting proxy, repeater, scanner, active vs passive scanning
bytes.title: Snyk: Mastering Burp Suite with Snyk's Vulnerability Scanner
bytes.body: When using Snyk's vulnerability scanner, you can integrate Burp Suite as an intercepting proxy to identify potential vulnerabilities in your applications. Burp Suite's repeater enables you to send repeated requests to the server, while the scanner analyzes the responses for security weaknesses. However, Snyk's scanner operates in two modes: active and passive scanning. Active scanning involves sending requests to the server to identify vulnerabilities, whereas passive scanning merely analyzes the server's responses without sending additional requests.

bytes.topic: SonarQube — code quality + security, quality gates, hotspots vs issues
bytes.title: Snyk: Unlocking SonarQube Quality Gates with Snyk's API
bytes.body: Snyk's API allows you to integrate SonarQube quality gates directly into your CI/CD pipeline. By doing so, you can ensure that only high-quality codebase is deployed to production. To achieve this, create a Snyk project and retrieve the SonarQube quality gate ID. Then, use the Snyk API to create a new quality gate configuration and link it to your SonarQube instance. Finally, use the Snyk API to trigger the quality gate and receive the results in your pipeline.
bytes.code_snippet:
snyk login
snyk project init
snyk config sonarqube quality-gate create -h
snyk config sonarqube quality-gate link -h
snyk config sonarqube quality-gate trigger -h

bytes.topic: Secrets Management — never commit secrets, rotation, detection with truffleHog/gitleaks
bytes.title: Snyk: Never Commit Secrets with TruffleHog and Gitleaks
bytes.body: Snyk's vulnerability scanner can detect secrets stored in your code repository. Use TruffleHog and Gitleaks to scan your repository for secrets and rotate them with Snyk's Secret Manager. Integrate TruffleHog into your Snyk workflow by configuring a custom Snyk CLI command. Use Snyk's Secret Manager to rotate sensitive data, like API keys, and store them securely.

bytes.topic: Dependency Vulnerabilities — CVE scanning, Snyk, npm audit, update strategies
Semgrep — rule writing, community rules, custom patterns, CI integration
bytes.title: Snyk Semgrep: Crafting Custom Rules with Patterns and CI Integration

bytes.topic: Shift Left Security — integrating security checks early in the development lifecycle
bytes.title: Integrating Snyk Security Checks Early in Your CI/CD Pipeline
bytes.body: Snyk's Open Source Security, Code Scanning, and Dependency Vulnerability checks can be seamlessly integrated into your CI/CD pipeline using Snyk's API. By utilizing the Snyk CLI and the `snyk test` command, you can automate security checks early in the development lifecycle. This allows developers to fix vulnerabilities and prevent them from being introduced into production. For example, you can use the Snyk API to scan your dependencies and receive a report on vulnerable packages. You can then use this report to update your dependencies and ensure that your application is secure.

bytes.topic: Threat Modeling — STRIDE, identifying trust boundaries, attack surface mapping
bytes.title: snyk: Threat Modeling with STRIDE and Trust Boundaries
bytes.body: Snyk's Vulnerability Database (VDB) provides a comprehensive threat modeling framework based on the STRIDE attack model. To identify trust boundaries, use the `snyk vulnerability list` command with the `--trust-boundary` flag. This flag highlights vulnerabilities that compromise sensitive data or system trust. For example, to identify vulnerabilities in the `my-app` package with trust boundary issues, run `snyk vulnerability list --trust-boundary my-app`. This will output a list of vulnerabilities with trust boundary concerns. To visualize your attack surface, use Snyk's Dependency Graph feature. This visual representation helps you identify vulnerable dependencies and their impact on your application.

bytes.topic: Snyk — developer-first security, fix PRs, container scanning, IaC scanning
Security Headers — CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy
bytes.title: Snyk: Bolstering App Security with Security Headers
bytes.body:
Snyk's Vulnerability Database (VDB) includes over 30,000 security headers, many of which can be enforced using Snyk's Open-Source Integration (OSI). To leverage these headers, navigate to your Snyk Project, select 'Security', and then 'Headers'. Here, you can configure policies for Content Security Policy (CSP), HTTP Strict Transport Security (HSTS), X-Frame-Options, and Referrer-Policy. For instance, to implement CSP, add the following policy: 

```typescript
snyk security headers policy add --policy-name my-csp-policy --policy-type content-security-policy --directive "default-src 'self';
object-src 'none';
frame-src 'none';"
```

This will create a policy named 'my-csp-policy' with the specified directives. You can then apply this policy to your project by running `snyk security headers apply --policy my-csp-policy`.

bytes.topic: Logging for Security — what to log, what not to log (PII), SIEM integration
bytes.title: snyk — Logging Security-Relevant Events: Best Practices

bytes.topic: Snyk — developer-first security, fix PRs, container scanning, IaC scanning
bytes.title: Snyk — Fix Vulnerable Dependencies in Pull Requests with Snyk CLI
bytes.body: Use Snyk CLI to scan your repository and create a Snyk project. Then, configure Snyk to monitor your project and trigger automatic vulnerability tests for pull requests. With the Snyk CLI, you can also scan dependencies in Docker containers and Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) files. To fix vulnerabilities in a pull request, run `snyk test` to identify issues, and `snyk fix` to apply patches. You can also use the Snyk CLI to generate a report on the security vulnerabilities in your project.
bytes.code_snippet:
snyk test
snyk fix
git add .
git commit -m ': fix vulnerabilities'
git push
